value.session_context: Friday close for the US cash-equity week; AP reported final index closes and weekly changes. The session capped a volatile week driven by AI-stock selling, with the Nasdaq Composite booking its fifth straight decline.

text: Friday, June 26, 2026 was a regular US market session for the cash-equity and options analysis basis used here. AP published final Friday closes for the S&P 500, Dow, Nasdaq Composite, and Russell 2000, and reported weekly performance through the close. Cboe options settled normally. The session capped a volatile week in which AI-linked stocks dragged the Nasdaq to its fifth straight decline even as the broader market showed better internal breadth.

value.context: Mixed close: S&P 500 and Nasdaq slipped while Dow and Russell 2000 also finished narrowly. Nearly two out of every three S&P 500 stocks rose, but AI-linked losses kept the cap-weighted index down. Weekly split: Nasdaq -4.6%, S&P -2.0%, Dow +0.6%, Russell +1.0%.

text: AP reported a mixed June 26 U.S. close: the S&P 500 slipped 3.47 points, or less than 0.1%, to 7,354.02; the Dow fell 44.51 points, or 0.1%, to 51,876.11; the Nasdaq Composite lost 60.99 points, or 0.2%, to 25,297.62 (its fifth straight decline); and the Russell 2000 rose 2.23 points, or 0.1%, to 3,010.08. For the week, the S&P 500 fell 2.0%, the Nasdaq fell 4.6%, the Dow rose 0.6%, and the Russell 2000 gained 1.0%. Despite the cap-weighted decline, AP reported that nearly two out of every three S&P 500 stocks rose, showing that the losses were concentrated in large AI-linked names rather than spreading broadly.

subject.name: CNN Fear & Greed Index
subject.source_basis: CNN Fear & Greed page corroborated by Finhacker.cz CNN-tracker

value.cnn_headline_text: extreme fear is driving the US market
value.methodology_note: CNN Stock Price Breadth indicator still reflects the McClellan Summation Index methodology change noted in prior reports
value.freshness_note: CNN's 25 is a post-close June 26 snapshot (5:23 PM ET). Finhacker's 26 reflects the June 24 post-close value. The one-point CNN-Finhacker gap (25 vs 26) is a freshness artifact, not a methodological disagreement.

market_session: latest available readings at collection time after the June 26 close
text: The Fear & Greed Index deepened in fear territory on June 26. CNN's page displayed 25 (fear) with the headline text 'extreme fear is driving the US market', 'Previous close 25', '1 week ago 37', '1 month ago 60', '1 year ago 63', and 'Last updated Jun 26 at 5:23:14 PM ET'. Finhacker.cz's CNN-tracker showed 26 (fear) with a data-updated timestamp of 'Jun 24, 2026, 8:00 PM ET'. The composite slipped one point from June 25's 26 to June 26's 25 on CNN, and is down 12 points from one month ago. Four of the five visible CNN sub-indicators were labeled fear or extreme fear at the time of access: market momentum (fear), stock price strength (fear, NYSE net new 52-week highs/lows 1.47%), stock price breadth (extreme fear, McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,408.51), and put/call options (extreme fear, 5-day average 0.69). Only market volatility was neutral (VIX below its 50-day moving average). The deepening of the put/call sub-indicator from 'fear' on June 25 to 'extreme fear' on June 26 is the notable day-over-day shift, consistent with the fifth consecutive Nasdaq decline and the persistent AI-stock overhang.

vix9d.freshness_note: June 26 close not available on Yahoo Finance at collection time; latest available is June 25

value.curve_date_basis: latest available: VIX from 2026-06-26 close; VIX9D through VIX1Y from 2026-06-25 close
value.intraday_range_note: VIX opened at 19.70, spiked to 20.72 in the morning selloff, then steadily eased to close at 18.20-low-18.41 as oil fell and yields eased into the close

nine_day_thirty_day_spread.note: VIX9D-to-VIX gap narrowed from 0.97 to 0.49 points using the latest available VIX9D; the true June 26 gap may differ if VIX9D also eased

market_session: Cboe published volatility-index data for the regular US session

calculation.method: VIX one-day percent change = (18.41 - 18.89) / 18.89 * 100 = -2.54%; intraday range 18.20-20.72; curve compares Cboe close fields for VIX (June 26) and VIX9D/VIX3M/VIX6M/VIX1Y (latest available June 25)

text: Yahoo Finance historical data showed VIX closing at 18.41 on June 26, down 0.48 points or 2.54% from 18.89 on June 25, with OHLC of open 19.70, high 20.72, low 18.20, close 18.41. Google Finance, MarketWatch, and WSJ all corroborate the 18.41 close. The intraday pattern is notable: VIX opened higher at 19.70, spiked to 20.72 during the morning AI-stock selloff, then steadily eased through the afternoon as Brent crude fell 3.8% and the 10-year Treasury yield dropped to 4.37%, closing near the session low. The Cboe volatility curve remained in contango and upward sloping: VIX at 18.41 (June 26), VIX9D at 17.92 (June 25 latest), VIX3M at 20.33 (June 25 latest), VIX6M at 22.35 (June 25 latest), and VIX1Y at 23.50 (June 25 latest). The VIX9D-to-VIX gap narrowed to approximately 0.49 points from June 25's 0.97-point spread (using latest available VIX9D). The longer tenors all eased slightly on June 25 (VIX3M -0.20%, VIX6M -0.22%, VIX1Y -1.48%), keeping the curve in orderly contango. The sharp spot-vol decline, despite the cap-weighted S&P 500's fifth straight decline, reflects the market's recognition that the selling remained concentrated in AI-linked mega-caps while breadth was improving and macro tail risks (oil, rates) eased.

value.interpretation: Two-tier pattern persists: single-stock options lean bullish (more calls than puts) while index flow leans defensive (more puts than calls). The total ratio dropped below 1.0 for the first time since June 12, but the index and SPX ratios remain above 1.0, confirming institutional tail-hedging alongside retail bullishness in single names.

market_session: Cboe end-of-day options statistics for the regular US session
text: Cboe end-of-day options statistics for June 26 showed a two-tier put/call pattern. The total put/call ratio was 0.99, with equity put/call at 0.67, index put/call at 1.18, exchange-traded products put/call at 1.14, VIX put/call at 0.57, and SPX+SPXW put/call at 1.23. Total options volume was 13,853,230 contracts (call 6,968,999 / put 6,884,231). Compared to June 25 (total 1.02, equity 0.61, index 1.16, ETP 1.38, SPX 1.31), the total ratio dropped below 1.0 for the first time since June 12, the ETP ratio fell sharply from 1.38 to 1.14, and the SPX ratio eased from 1.31 to 1.23. The pattern - bullish single-stock options flow (equity P/C 0.67, well below 1.0) alongside persistent defensive index hedging (index P/C 1.18, SPX P/C 1.23) - confirms that institutions continue buying tail protection via index products while retail positioning remains bullish on individual names. The CNN Fear & Greed 5-day average put/call ratio of 0.69 (extreme fear zone) is a different construction from the Cboe end-of-day ratios but points in the same direction: options-market sentiment is cautious overall, with the defensive bid concentrated in index products.

value.headline: Optimism Leaps
value.freshness_note: This is the latest available AAII weekly survey for the June 26 report date. The survey period closed Wednesday 6/24 and results were published Thursday 6/25. The next survey (week ending 7/1) will not be published until July 2. The survey does not capture Friday June 26 cash-session action.

market_session: latest AAII weekly survey published for the June 26 report date

calculation.method: Bull-bear spread = bullish 44.9% minus bearish 36.1% = +8.8 percentage points; prior spread = 36.6 - 39.4 = -2.8 pp; one-week swing = 8.8 - (-2.8) = +11.6 pp

text: The latest AAII sentiment survey (week ending 6/24/2026, published Thursday June 25) remains the most recent retail-investor sentiment gauge available for the June 26 report. Bullish sentiment was 44.9%, neutral 18.9%, and bearish 36.1%, leaving a bull-bear spread of +8.8 percentage points - the first above-historical-average reading in six weeks. The swing from the prior week's -2.8 pp spread was an 11.6-percentage-point one-week jump. However, the survey period closed before the June 26 cash-session action, so the bullish print does not reflect the Nasdaq's fifth straight decline or the further deepening of the Fear & Greed Index to 25. The AAII survey captured the improvement in mood driven by the Iran de-escalation, the Micron-guidance-led chip rally, and the in-line May PCE release, none of which fully offset the late-week AI-stock pressure in the composite sentiment indices.

value.data_freshness_note: ICI's latest combined-flow release (published June 24 for the week ended June 17) predates the June 23-26 AI-stock selloff; the next ICI release (week ended June 24) had not been published at collection time. ETF.com's June 22 weekly article also predates the late-week selloff. Positioning context, not real-time flow.

market_session: latest fund-flow releases available for the June 26 report date
text: The latest available fund-flow data provides positioning context heading into the June 26 session rather than a real-time flow gauge. ICI's combined-flow release (published June 24 for the week ended June 17) showed $66.23 billion of total long-term inflows, including $92.03 billion of ETF net issuance and $25.80 billion of mutual fund outflows. Equity funds took in $55.75 billion (domestic $49.60B, world $6.15B), bond funds took in $16.46 billion. ETF.com's June 22 weekly article reported $119.83 billion of total ETF inflows led by $83.62 billion into U.S. equity ETFs, with IVV (+$30.40B), SPYM (+$12.87B), XLK (+$8.33B), and SMH (+$7.44B) the top creations and SPY (-$29.62B) and VOO (-$14.42B) the top redemptions. Both data releases predate the June 23-26 AI-stock selloff, so they capture the post-SpaceX-IPO risk appetite but not the late-week de-risking. The SPY/VOO redemptions alongside IVV/SPYM creations suggest structural rotation (share-class creation flow) rather than outright equity abandonment.

value.brent_note: below its level before the US and Israel attacked Iran
value.macro_theme: Oil and yields eased (risk-positive), but Fed hawkish shift and above-4% inflation cap the upside (risk-negative). Net: classic late-week relief rally in oil/rates, not a clean risk-on regime change.

text: AP reported Brent crude down 3.8% to $72.60, below where it stood before the US and Israel attacked Iran, and the 10-year Treasury yield eased to 4.37% from 4.40% late Thursday. AP also cited a decline in one-year US consumer inflation expectations to 4.6% from 4.8% in May. The macro relief from falling oil and yields did not remove Fed risk: CNBC reported that Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari changed his outlook from one rate cut by year-end to one rate hike by year-end, citing Middle East supply risk, tariffs, energy and fertilizer disruptions, and data-center investment pressure. CNBC also reported May headline PCE inflation at 4.1% (highest since April 2023) and core PCE at 3.4%. The cross-asset price action - Brent down sharply, yields easing, consumer inflation expectations easing - is consistent with a late-week relief bid in risk assets, but the Fed backdrop remains hawkish and inflation is still above 4%, capping the upside for sentiment.

subject.label: spot vol easing while composite fear deepens - bifurcated sentiment regime

value.cash_equity_tape: Mixed close: S&P -<0.1%, Nasdaq -0.2% (5th straight decline), Dow -0.1%, Russell +0.1%. Nearly two out of three S&P 500 stocks rose. AI-linked losses (Micron -6.7%, ON Semi -23.7%) outweighed broad gains.
value.survey_sentiment: AAII Bullish 44.9% / +8.8 pp spread (stale: published 6/25, pre-Friday action)
value.volatility: VIX -2.54% to 18.41 (intraday range 18.20-20.72); VIX9D/VIX3M/VIX6M/VIX1Y latest available June 25; curve in contango
value.options: two-tier: equity P/C 0.67 (bullish single names), index P/C 1.18, SPX P/C 1.23 (defensive hedging); total P/C below 1.0 for first time since June 12
value.flows: Latest available flows (ICI week ended June 17, ETF.com June 22) pre-date the AI-stock selloff; $83.62B into US equity ETFs in the SpaceX-IPO week
value.fear_greed: CNN 25 (fear), down from 26 on June 25 and 37 a week ago; Finhacker 26 (June 24 data); put/call sub-indicator deepened from fear to extreme fear
value.risk_read: The bifurcation between spot vol (falling sharply as oil eased) and composite sentiment (deepening to 25 as put/call and breadth indicators deteriorate) is the defining signal. The market is pricing out near-term event risk while the cumulative damage to breadth and options positioning keeps the Fear & Greed Index in the fear zone.

market_session: regular US cash-equity session synthesis from latest available sentiment indicators
text: The best synthesis for June 26 is 'spot vol easing while composite fear deepens - a bifurcated sentiment regime.' VIX dropped 2.54% to 18.41, with the intraday range (18.20-20.72) showing that the opening AI-stock panic was priced out through the afternoon as Brent crude fell 3.8% and the 10-year yield eased to 4.37%. The Cboe total put/call ratio dropped below 1.0 for the first time since June 12, and the ETP and SPX put/call ratios both eased from June 25 levels. But the Fear & Greed Index slipped further to 25 (fear), the put/call sub-indicator deepened from 'fear' to 'extreme fear' (5-day average 0.69), and stock price breadth remained in 'extreme fear' territory (McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,408.51). The divergence between declining spot volatility and deepening composite fear is the defining signal of this cycle: the market is pricing out near-term event risk (oil easing, yields easing, VIX falling), but the cumulative damage to market breadth and options positioning from the Nasdaq's five-session decline keeps the composite sentiment index in the fear zone. The AAII survey's 44.9% bullish print is now two days stale and captures the Iran-de-escalation and chip-rally optimism rather than the Friday action. The regime is not a clean risk-on (composite sentiment is at 25, Fed is hawkish, inflation is above 4%) and not the June 23 panic (VIX fell, oil fell, breadth was positive at the stock level). It is a late-week relief bid layered over a structurally cautious positioning environment, with the institutional tail-hedge bid in index options persisting even as single-stock options flow stays bullish.
